caveats: a: OEWS combines computers with storage and peripheral equipment.
a: Large OEM occupation mixes may differ from LMM rugged, custom, and specialist system builders.
rho: Revenue or throughput gains are not all labor substitution.
rho: Implementation economics differ sharply between high-volume PCs and low-volume custom servers or rugged systems.
e: Establishments are not firms.
e: The 25.32% Computers/Peripherals margin bridge may overstate EBITDA for assembly-heavy or competitively priced hardware firms.
s5: The source is not industry-specific and does not measure completed control transfers.
s5: Acqui-hires and product-line sales may not preserve the lens's operating business.
q: Dell is far larger and has different purchasing power and channel economics.
q: Commodity PCs, AI servers, rugged computers, and bespoke systems have materially different retention.
d5: BLS output includes storage and peripherals and is value rather than physical units.
d5: Dell's AI-server growth is not representative of all LMM domestic manufacturers.
o: Cloud services and imports can displace domestic manufacturers without making software the physical supplier.
o: Operator requirement does not imply unchanged employment.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat hardware sales and configured system projects to enterprises, governments, data centers, distributors, and specialist users, priced per unit or configuration with component, warranty, support, volume, and channel terms.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive manufacturing units, pure resellers and retailers, software-only companies, component makers, repair-only firms, embedded-computer machinery manufacturers, passive entities, and firms without transferable operations.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. firms repeatedly designing, configuring, assembling, integrating, testing, and selling electronic computers, including servers, workstations, rugged or specialized computers, to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures broader NAICS 334100, showing a high-wage mix spanning management, computer, engineering, sales, office, production, maintenance, and logistics work. NIST documents manufacturing AI uses in quality, maintenance, forecasting, scheduling, and resource management. I translate those broader occupations and use cases into wage-weighted remaining exposure for computer makers; heavy engineering and commercial compensation raises exposure, while existing design, test, and factory automation lowers the not-yet-automated share.
caveats: OEWS combines computers with storage and peripheral equipment.
Large OEM occupation mixes may differ from LMM rugged, custom, and specialist system builders.
a.change_evidence: A representative 334111 task-and-wage study separating existing automation from remaining engineering, sales, support, production, test, quality, procurement, and administrative hours would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can accelerate product configuration, engineering documentation, test analysis, visual inspection, failure diagnosis, demand and inventory planning, sourcing, quoting, customer support, and administration. Physical assembly, handling, rework, certification, secure supply-chain control, and accountable engineering remain.

d5.bridge: BLS projects broader 334100 real output from $43.5 billion in 2024 to $51.0 billion in 2034, a 1.6% annual rate. Dell reports strong server growth driven by AI-optimized offerings alongside weaker consumer PCs and pricing. I convert the broader output trend and product-cycle evidence to a five-year constant-quality demand ratio for computer manufacturing; AI servers may make the proxy overstate LMM-accessible growth, while custom edge and secure systems may outperform PCs.
caveats: BLS output includes storage and peripherals and is value rather than physical units.
Dell's AI-server growth is not representative of all LMM domestic manufacturers.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year IDC or Gartner U.S. shipment forecast by server, PC, workstation, rugged, edge, and custom-system segment, reconciled to domestic production rather than vendor revenue, would materially improve the estimate.
d5.rationale: AI infrastructure, data growth, edge computing, cybersecurity, sovereign and secure supply chains, and refresh cycles support demand. Cloud concentration, offshore manufacturing, virtualization, longer device lives, commoditization, and volatile enterprise spending create downside.

caveats: Establishments are not firms.
The 25.32% Computers/Peripherals margin bridge may overstate EBITDA for assembly-heavy or competitively priced hardware firms.
e.change_evidence: A named review of the 61 candidates covering ultimate ownership, manufacturing substance, external recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, certifications, export exposure, and transferable IP would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Census reports 212 employer establishments and the frozen estimate identifies 61 firms in the EBITDA band. Independent custom server, workstation, rugged, and specialist computer builders plausibly fit, but large OEM sites, captive units, foreign-controlled operations, resellers misclassified by assembly, weak-margin firms, and businesses dependent on a few contracts reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 334111 ($14,560,722,526) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 334111 ($12,488,255,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2532 (margins.json[334]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Computers/Peripherals EBITDA/Sales 25.32%); revenue/employee $894,062 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 334111); class <5: 79 firms, avg revenue/firm $791,873 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $200,502 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 37 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,374,568 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $854,441 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,710,360 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,445,863 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,231,194 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,869,338 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $143,613,524 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,362,944 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $444,730,105 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $112,605,663 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 61

caveats: Cloud services and imports can displace domestic manufacturers without making software the physical supplier.
Operator requirement does not imply unchanged employment.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of broad customer self-assembly, software or cloud substitution eliminating specific hardware categories, or autonomous contract manufacturing that removes the lens operator would lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Customers still require a physical and accountable maker to source, integrate, test, certify, warrant, secure, and deliver computer hardware. AI can automate internal work and enable cloud substitution, but it cannot itself supply the remaining physical systems within five years.

q.bridge: Dell reports competitive client pricing, falling average selling prices, component-cost effects, and lower gross-margin percentage as AI-server mix increased. I translate these large-OEM disclosures to LMM computer manufacturers: transparent components, bids, channels, and frequent refreshes push efficiency gains toward customers, while specialization, integration, support, qualification, and proprietary designs can protect more value than at a commodity OEM.
caveats: Dell is far larger and has different purchasing power and channel economics.
Commodity PCs, AI servers, rugged computers, and bespoke systems have materially different retention.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm contracts, bid histories, configuration premiums, renewal and discount data, component pass-through clauses, and gross-margin persistence after automation projects would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Commodity PCs and standard servers face rapid repricing and visible component costs. Ruggedization, secure supply chains, custom configuration, certifications, integration, short lead times, and support can retain a meaningful minority of implemented benefit.

caveats: Revenue or throughput gains are not all labor substitution.
Implementation economics differ sharply between high-volume PCs and low-volume custom servers or rugged systems.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at U.S. computer assemblers reporting cycle time, first-pass yield, test escapes, engineering-change errors, inventory, service resolution, model overrides, and labor hours saved would narrow the estimate.
rho.rationale: Digital engineering artifacts, bills of material, test logs, support tickets, and structured ERP data favor implementation. Fast product cycles, supplier changes, cybersecurity, export controls, customer-specific qualification, hallucination risk, legacy systems, and hardware accountability constrain deployment.

s5.bridge: SBA reports that 47% of family-business owners expecting retirement within five years lack a successor. I treat this as broad succession pressure, then discount it because it is cross-industry and because technology firms may instead raise capital, sell assets, close, or be acquired for IP without a qualifying transfer of an eligible operating firm.
caveats: The source is not industry-specific and does not measure completed control transfers.
Acqui-hires and product-line sales may not preserve the lens's operating business.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and sector transaction records matched to eligible 334111 firms, plus owner-age and succession interviews, would replace the proxy.
s5.rationale: Founder-led specialist builders can face succession or strategic-sale pressure, while rapid obsolescence, supplier concentration, customer qualification, working capital, and dependence on technical founders can prevent transfer.

principal_driver: Digitized engineering, test, support, and supply-chain data make multiple high-wage workflows AI-addressable.
principal_weakness: Transparent components and fierce hardware pricing can pass efficiency gains through before the operator recovers implementation cost.

caveats: S1's exact Census profile was reachable only as a JavaScript shell in the validator's text retriever, so its definition and 212-establishment statistic were not directly inspectable; this is not material because e is already an ESTIMATE, o is judgmental, and the frozen firm-count input rather than this establishment count drives the base availability calculation.

S5's exact Census AIES table was reachable only as a JavaScript shell in the validator's text retriever, so the stated sales-or-shipments and operating-expense figures were not directly inspectable; those figures are contextual rather than the numeric basis of the e estimate and do not change the base tier or action.

The a primitive combines a broader 334100 occupational mix with generic manufacturing AI use cases rather than measuring wage-weighted remaining exposure for 334111 LMM firms. The record labels this PROXY, states the bridge and error directions, and provides a wide range.

The e primitive translates establishments and a frozen margin-bridged firm count into eligible transferable firms without a named-candidate review. It is appropriately labeled ESTIMATE and is already the base score's weakest-link factor.

The q and d5 bridges extrapolate from Dell, whose scale, purchasing power, channels, and AI-server mix differ materially from LMM specialist computer builders. The mismatch is disclosed, the ranges are broad, and correcting the broad demand growth proxy alone would not change the capped base D factor or the model-conditioned action.

The s5 bridge rests on a 2018 SBA blog repeating a cross-industry SCORE succession statistic, not observed completed control transfers in 334111. The record explicitly labels it PROXY and discounts it, so this weakness does not warrant rejection.

sources_audited.note: The exact Census profile URL resolves, but the reopened page exposes only a JavaScript-required shell in the validator's text retriever, so the 212-establishment figure and definition could not be directly inspected.

The BLS page is explicitly the May 2023 national occupation-and-wage table for broader NAICS 334100 and shows the cited management, computer, engineering, sales, office, production, maintenance, and logistics occupation groups.

NIST documents the cited manufacturing AI use cases in maintenance, quality, forecasting, inventory and resource management, scheduling, supply chains, inspection, and digital twins, together with data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-integration barriers.

Dell's fiscal 2025 Form 10-K supports strong AI-optimized server demand and growth, weaker consumer-client performance, competitive pricing pressure, component and vendor economics, support revenue, and lower gross-margin percentages associated with AI-server mix and client pricing.

The exact Census AIES table URL resolves, but the reopened page exposes only a JavaScript-required shell in the validator's text retriever, so the stated 2023 sales-or-shipments and operating-expense values could not be directly inspected.
BLS Table 2.11 lists broader NAICS 334100 real output of $43.5 billion in 2024 and $51.0 billion in 2034, with a 1.6% compound annual growth rate.

The SBA blog reports that 47% of owners expecting to retire within five years lack a successor; the record accurately treats this older, cross-industry, secondary statistic only as a broad succession proxy.
